title: Apply: fossils and rock layers
description: Apply your understanding of fossils and rock layers in this set of free practice questions aligned to NGSS standards.
topic_path: Middle school Earth and space science - NGSS

assessment_items.question_content:
The diagram below shows a cross section of layers of sedimentary rock. The rock layers are undisturbed.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which layer is the youngest?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To solve this problem, let’s use the law of superposition. According to this law, in undisturbed layers of sedimentary rock, the youngest layer is at the *top*.
In this case, the layer at the top of the diagram is the limestone layer.
So, the limestone layer is the youngest.

options.alt: A geologic cross section shows 3 horizontal rock layers. From top to bottom, the layers are: 1. Limestone. The limestone layer contains 1 fossil. 2. Shale. The shale layer contains no fossils. 3. Sandstone. The sandstone layer contains 3 fossils.

assessment_items.question_content:
The diagram below shows a cross section of layers of sedimentary rock. The rock layers are undisturbed.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which layer is the oldest?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To solve this problem, let’s use the law of superposition. According to this law, in undisturbed layers of sedimentary rock, the oldest layer is at the *bottom*.
In this case, the layer at the bottom of the diagram is the shale layer.
So, the shale layer is the oldest.

options.alt: A geologic cross section shows 3 horizontal rock layers. From top to bottom, the layers are: 1. Siltstone. The siltstone layer contains 1 fossil. 2. Sandstone. The sandstone layer contains 2 fossils. 3. Shale. The shale layer contains 1 fossil.

assessment_items.question_content:
The diagram below shows a cross section of layers of sedimentary rock. The rock layers are undisturbed.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which layer is the oldest?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To solve this problem, let’s use the law of superposition. According to this law, in undisturbed layers of sedimentary rock, the oldest layer is at the *bottom*.
In this case, the layer at the bottom of the diagram is the sandstone layer.
So, the sandstone layer is the oldest.

options.alt: A geologic cross section shows 3 horizontal rock layers. From top to bottom, the layers are: 1. Shale. The shale layer contains 2 fossils. 2. Limestone. The limestone layer contains 1 fossil. 3. Sandstone. The sandstone layer contains no fossils.

assessment_items.question_content:
The diagram below shows a cross section containing sedimentary rock layers, an igneous intrusion, and a fault.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which statement best compares the relative ages of the intrusion and the fault?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To solve this problem, let’s use the law of crosscutting relationships. According to this law, when two geologic features intersect, the feature that cuts through the other is *younger*.
In this case, the diagram shows the intrusion cutting through the fault.
So, the intrusion is younger than the fault.

options.alt: A geologic cross section shows 3 horizontal rock layers, an intrusion, and a fault. The fault appears as a slanted line that cuts through all 3 layers. The portions of the layers on either side of the fault line do not align. The intrusion appears as a mound-like clump. It cuts into portions of the bottom 2 layers and through the fault.

choices.content: The intrusion is younger than the fault.
The fault is younger than the intrusion.
The intrusion and the fault are the same age.

assessment_items.question_content:
The diagram below shows a cross section containing sedimentary rock layers, an igneous intrusion, and a fault.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which statement best compares the relative ages of the intrusion and the fault?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To solve this problem, let’s use the law of crosscutting relationships. According to this law, when two geologic features intersect, the feature that cuts through the other is *younger*.
In this case, the diagram shows the fault cutting through the intrusion.
So, the fault is younger than the intrusion.

options.alt: A geologic cross section shows 3 horizontal rock layers, an intrusion, and a fault. The intrusion appears as a vertical clump that cuts through the bottom 2 layers and into the top layer. The fault appears as a slanted line that cuts through all 3 layers and the intrusion. The portions of the layers and the intrusion on either side of the fault line do not align.

choices.content: The intrusion is younger than the fault.
The fault is younger than the intrusion.
The intrusion and the fault are the same age.

assessment_items.question_content:
The diagram below shows a cross section of sedimentary rock layers containing two igneous intrusions, one granite and one basalt.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which statement best compares the relative ages of the granite and basalt intrusions?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To solve this problem, let’s use the law of crosscutting relationships. According to this law, when two geologic features intersect, the feature that cuts through the other is *younger*.
In this case, the diagram shows the basalt intrusion cutting through the granite intrusion.
So, the basalt intrusion is younger than the granite intrusion.

options.alt: A geologic cross section shows 4 horizontal rock layers, a granite intrusion, and a basalt intrusion. The granite intrusion appears as a mound-like clump that cuts into the bottom 3 layers. The basalt intrusion appears as a vertical stripe that cuts through all 4 layers and the granite intrusion.

choices.content: The granite intrusion is younger than the basalt intrusion.
The basalt intrusion is younger than the granite intrusion.
The granite and basalt intrusions are the same age.

assessment_items.question_content:
The diagrams below show the sedimentary rock layers found at two different locations $20\,\text{km}$ apart. Letters A, B, and C represent different index fossils present in the layers.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which two layers are approximately the same age?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Index fossils can be used to determine the relative ages of rock layers. If the same index fossil is present in rock layers in different locations, it is likely that the layers formed around the same time.
According to the diagram, the same index fossil (fossil B) is present in both layer 3 and layer 4.
So, layer 3 and layer 4 are approximately the same age.

options.alt: A geologic cross section shows 2 rock columns, each with 3 horizontal rock layers. Starting from the surface and going down on the left column, the layers are: Layer 1. This layer contains no index fossils. Layer 2. This layer contains index fossil A. Layer 3. This layer contains index fossil B. Starting from the surface and going down on the right column, the layers are: Layer 4. This layer contains index fossil B. Layer 5. This layer contains index fossil C. Layer 6. This layer contains no index fossils.

assessment_items.question_content:
The diagrams below show the sedimentary rock layers found at two different locations $15\,\text{km}$ apart. Letters A, B, and C represent different index fossils present in the layers.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which two layers are approximately the same age?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Index fossils can be used to determine the relative ages of rock layers. If the same index fossil is present in rock layers in different locations, it is likely that the layers formed around the same time.
According to the diagram, the same index fossil (fossil A) is present in both layer 1 and layer 5.
So, layer 1 and layer 5 are approximately the same age.

options.alt: A geologic cross section shows 2 rock columns, each with 3 horizontal rock layers. Starting from the surface and going down on the left column, the layers are: Layer 1. This layer contains index fossil A. Layer 2. This layer contains index fossil B. Layer 3. This layer contains no index fossils. Starting from the surface and going down on the right column, the layers are: Layer 4. This layer contains index fossil C. Layer 5. This layer contains index fossil A. Layer 6. This layer contains no index fossils.

assessment_items.question_content:
The diagrams below show the sedimentary rock layers found at two different locations $25\,\text{km}$ apart. Letters A, B, and C represent different index fossils present in the layers.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which two layers are approximately the same age?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Index fossils can be used to determine the relative ages of rock layers. If the same index fossil is present in rock layers in different locations, it is likely that the layers formed around the same time.
According to the diagram, the same index fossil (fossil A) is present in both layer 2 and layer 6.
So, layer 2 and layer 6 are approximately the same age.

options.alt: A geologic cross section shows 2 rock columns, each with 3 horizontal rock layers. Starting from the surface and going down on the left column, the layers are: Layer 1. This layer contains no index fossils. Layer 2. This layer contains index fossil A. Layer 3. This layer contains index fossil B. Starting from the surface and going down on the right column, the layers are: Layer 4. This layer contains no index fossils. Layer 5. This layer contains index fossil C. Layer 6. This layer contains index fossil A.

assessment_items.question_content:
The diagram below shows the fossils contained in layers of sedimentary rock in a certain region. The rock layers are undisturbed.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What do the fossils reveal about the environment in the region over time?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To solve this problem, let’s examine the types of fossils in each rock layer in the diagram. Remember that fossils in lower layers are *older* than fossils in layers toward the top.

Looking at the diagram, we can see that the lowest layer contains fish and shell fossils, which come from organisms that live in water. The next lowest layer contains a fossil of a frog, which is an organism that lives both in water and on land. Finally, the top layer contains dinosaur footprint and leaf fossils, which come from organisms that live on land.
So, the fossils suggest that the environment has changed from aquatic to land over time.

options.alt: A geologic cross section shows 3 horizontal rock layers. The top layer contains 1 set of fossilized dinosaur footprints and 2 leaf fossils. The middle layer contains 1 frog fossil. The bottom layer contains 1 fish fossil and 1 shell fossil.

choices.content: It changed from aquatic to land.
It changed from land to aquatic.

assessment_items.question_content:
The diagram below shows the fossils contained in layers of sedimentary rock in a certain region. The rock layers are undisturbed.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What do the fossils reveal about the environment in the region over time?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To solve this problem, let’s examine the types of fossils in each rock layer in the diagram. Remember that fossils in lower layers are *older* than fossils in layers toward the top.

Looking at the diagram, we can see that the lowest layer contains leaf and dinosaur footprint fossils, which come from organisms that live on land. The next lowest layer contains a fossil of a frog, which is an organism that lives both in water and on land. Finally, the top layer contains shell and fish fossils, which come from organisms that live in water.
So, the fossils suggest that the environment has changed from land to aquatic over time.

options.alt: A geologic cross section shows 3 horizontal rock layers. The top layer contains 1 shell fossil and 1 fish fossil. The middle layer contains 1 frog fossil. The bottom layer contains 2 leaf fossils and 1 set of fossilized dinosaur footprints.

choices.content: It changed from aquatic to land.
It changed from land to aquatic.

assessment_items.question_content:
The diagram below shows the fossils contained in layers of sedimentary rock in a certain region. The rock layers are undisturbed.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What do the fossils reveal about the environment in the region over time?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To solve this problem, let’s examine the types of fossils in each rock layer in the diagram. Remember that fossils in lower layers are *older* than fossils in layers toward the top.

Looking at the diagram, we can see that the lowest layer contains fish and coral fossils, which come from organisms that live in the ocean. The next lowest layer contains a fossil of a crab, which is an organism that lives both in the ocean and on land. Finally, the top layer contains leaf and dinosaur footprint fossils, which come from organisms that live on land.
So, the fossils suggest that the environment has changed from marine to land over time.

options.alt: A geologic cross section shows 3 horizontal rock layers. The top layer contains 2 leaf fossils and 1 set of fossilized dinosaur footprints. The middle layer contains 1 crab fossil. The bottom layer contains 1 fish fossil and 1 coral fossil.

choices.content: It changed from marine to land.
It changed from land to marine.
